title: Labor Left pushes for hard cap on specialist doctor fees
full_text:
Fee increases for specialist doctors would be capped under a new policy rank-and-file Labor members are urging Health Minister Mark Butler to make law and which is being backed by private health insurers.
The change to Labor's platform, which will be adopted at the party's national conference later this month, would make it policy for the government to work with insurers to crack down on non-general practice specialists, who the amendment says engage in "cartel behaviour".
The amendment blames fees charged by specialist doctors, such as neurologists, cardiologists, and anaesthetists, for "increasingly making specialist care unaffordable for most Australians".
"Labor believes that corporate profits should not be made from illness and believes investing in a world-class public health system is the answer ending corporate greed in healthcare," said the change pushed by Victoria's Left faction, seen by The Australian Financial Review.
The government has referred the matter to a parliamentary inquiry that is due to report in October. Health Minister Mark Butler said reducing specialists' fees was a priority amid intensifying debate. "I've asked my department for advice on options to start to get this huge growth in out-of-pocket costs back under control."
The average out-of-pocket cost for a non-bulk-billed specialist consultation, which is not regulated in any way, soared from about $46 in 2010 to $126 in 2025, according to analysis published earlier this year by Monash University professor Anthony Scott.
Australian Taxation Office data showed specialists have the highest incomes in the country, with surgeons, anaesthetists, internal medical specialists, psychiatrists and other medical practitioners comprising five of the six top-earning occupations.
The push to overhaul their fees is being led by Julijana Todorovic, the assistant secretary of the Victorian Left faction and a bone cancer survivor, who said while Butler had done a great deal to improve healthcare, tackling non-GP specialist services was unfinished business. "It's greed in its purest form, and we have an opportunity to shift the dial and open up access for all Australians to the care they need."
Health insurer lobby group Private Healthcare Australia has been urging the federal government to take action on fees, arguing the out-of-pocket cost of seeing a specialist doctor has become so expensive many Australians are deferring critical medical care. Redbridge research found last year that nearly one in three Australians referred to a specialist were delaying or cancelling because of the cost.
Insurers have a commercial interest in getting specialist fees charged in hospitals lowered as they have to fund those costs.
"They have become one of the biggest barriers preventing Australians from accessing the healthcare they need," PHA chief executive Rachel David said on Sunday. "Since the pandemic, specialist fees have increased well above inflation and wage growth, leaving many patients with unaffordable out-of-pocket costs."
Currently, the 15 million Australians with private health insurance cannot claim consultation costs with specialists such as paediatricians, oncologists, psychiatrists and dermatologists if they take place outside hospitals.
"Health funds ... are prohibited by law from paying for consultations with specialists outside hospital," David said.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Ronald Mizen and Michael Smith
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
